critic_raw: The CNN-vs-random-forest land cover classification experiment has two core issues. First, the stratified random split on satellite imagery ignores spatial autocorrelation — adjacent image tiles in train/test sets inflate generalization metrics. Spatial blocking is the standard approach in geospatial ML. Second, accuracy as primary metric under class imbalance favors majority land cover classes, misaligning with the stated urban planning use case where minority classes are of highest interest.
all_issues_raised: Spatial autocorrelation leakage from random split on tiled imagery — spatial blocking required
Accuracy under class imbalance misaligns with urban planning use case

round_transcripts.critic: Two issues: (1) Random split ignores spatial autocorrelation — adjacent tiles in train/test inflate performance. (2) Accuracy under class imbalance favors majority classes, misaligning with urban planning stakeholder need.
round_transcripts.defender: Spatial autocorrelation in imagery is a well-established concern — random splits distribute proximate tiles across train/test, and the model has seen the neighborhood of test samples. Spatial blocking is required. On accuracy: for broad-scale mapping the dominant classes can be the primary interest, but the explicit urban planning use case makes weighted F1 more appropriate.

round_transcripts.critic: Both concerns conceded. Spatial leakage inflates results; accuracy metric misaligns with stated purpose.
round_transcripts.defender: Conceded. Spatial blocking and weighted F1 as primary metric are required. CNN vs. RF comparison direction may still hold after correction.

all_issues_adjudicated: Spatial autocorrelation leakage — performance estimates inflated, spatial blocking required
Accuracy primary metric misaligns with urban planning use case under class imbalance
